data.title: Uber's Ecosystem Bet: Scaling Strong But Time To Wait
data.description:
Uber (NYSE:UBER) is expected to report Q1 2025 numbers in the first week of May'25 and that will be closely monitored by investors and analysts. After turning profitable in 2023, Uber has shown consistent scaled growth that led to a rally in share prices till early 2024. Market action has been subdued since, as the market watches out for clues on the next move - and the quarterly results should well have some pointers.

Becoming Profitable

One of the concerns I have is timing fresh entries in UBER at this stage. Has the ship sailed? In Q2 2023, Uber reported its first operating profitability. The major contributor to that was a revenue explosion. Uber’s breakthrough revenue jump was already in motion in 2022. Mobility recovered in 2022 on post COVID reopening. Higher gross bookings benefited from surge pricing. Uber’s delivery segment also managed to hold on to the COVID boom and while order growth stagnated, Uber was managing a growth in revenue per order. Boosted by acquisition of Transplace, Uber Freight had the breakthrough year, doubling freight top line. The ability to scale without disproportionately burdening operational costs saw Uber live up to its promise and stocks soared. From mid 2022 to mid 2023 (Uber’s breakout period) Uber shares doubled.

What Uber did well after this breakthrough from macro tailwinds, was maintain a reasonable growth rate for the next few quarters. In 2024, Uber continued its upward move both in terms of revenue and operating profit. Stock prices mirrored this journey as Uber continued to show its ability to hold on to the growth levels at scale. Stock prices again gave a handsome ~80% return till about Q1 2024.

As the chart above shows, Q4 2024 did show a ~10% Q-o-Q jump in revenues. Let us look for early green shoots to see whether this revenue uptick has enough to pull stock prices upwards again after stagnation.

Eighteen percent increase in gross bookings in both mobility and delivery was noted in Q4 2024 vs Q4 2023. Average booking value increases meant mobility revenues grew by 25%, while delivery revenues grew by 21%. Freight remained at similar levels in 2024. Numbers were strong enough to push growth in EBITDA, gross bookings and free cash higher than the higher range of management guidance. Share prices did revert back to $80 around the Q4 results (gaining back ~33%). But have again paused around the last one year average of $75.

The reason for this recap is to recall the long term waves already past and priced in with significant moves in UBER. Even the recent high of Q4 2024 led to a sharp recovery. The fear of heights arises from the history of significant upmoves - it almost looks like Uber has already priced in all good reasons to invest. In that context, is the recent lull an opportunity or should we be concerned?

Macro Risks and Slowdown Concerns

I think markets are pricing in the drop in discretionary spending as the economy braces for a slowdown and tariffs start impacting consumer behavior. Q1 2025 may still report good numbers, but the path ahead remains uncertain. The tariff threat is certainly not impacting Uber directly on imports or production. There is no direct bearing on Uber’s balance sheet or income statement. However, costs will become higher for drivers. May lead to service quality drop, or even higher costs to customers who will be cutting down on discretionary spends anyway. The freight segment may remain exposed completely to shipping activity changes.

Capability to Back Valuation

As Uber shares price in the downturn, how have valuations travelled? After the initial trailing 12 month PE expansion around and beyond 100, trailing PE has largely climbed down consistently to ~15 throughout 2024. These are early days after turning profitable, so assume settling period for the valuation multiples. The forward PE of 30 is still expensive, compare this to Lyft whose forward PE multiple is 10.

To understand what the premium valuation of Uber expects, let us look at the rapid strides and capability development happening at Uber. Eventually, it will boil down to how much of the potential vs valuation balances out.

Total addressable market of the mobility leg is secure with lots of room left for expansion.

Uber’s offering is beyond its competitors’ passenger ground transportation offerings. The eco system lock-in via Uber One is showing up well and expanding since 2021. The multi product consumer spend growth per se is not exciting, although growth in number of multi product customers is. This Uber’s compounding offering benefits over other ridesharing competitors (single product vs multi).

Cross promotion benefits helps customer acquisition costs as well (Uber has proven that with revenue growth in 2024 at similar cost bases). While the multi product strategy looks a great one and working, almost justifying a premium valuation, I am concerned about customer stickiness in a lower spending environment. Unlike business that operate on transition costs, hurdles and contracts, Uber’s customers have little hurdle to switch to alternatives on each offering, unless offerings remain competitive.

As a first mover in the diversified logistics space, Uber has done well to drive in customer lock-in through the Uber One program. Drivers also find more value unlock in cross serving delivery with rideshare. The economics have started working and will give Uber an edge till cost pressures are significant enough for customers to look for alternatives.

The starter advantage is a self fueling cycle for the future, as more data means more AI enhanced solutions to work on – be it pricing, risk and safety, queuing, routing or even languages. Advertisements are also likely to deliver compounding revenues in the future. Advertisements are particularly an exciting prospect in tech retail where data leads to personalized targeting opportunities.

Autonomous offerings is another key area where Uber has partnered with AV only operators like Waymo. These partnerships are seeing good traction and is a huge bet for the future. But the partnerships are loosely coupled with Uber (partners viewing Uber as a marketplace to introduce their offerings), and if the ecosystem advantage wanes off in economic duress, partners could offer more value on their own and drift off after initial customer reach is enabled by Uber.

Management Guidance and Analyst Estimates

Management’s guidance is extremely buoyant. With a three-year gross bookings growth of 15-20%, adjusted EBITDA growth of ~40% and Free Cash flow margin with respect to adjusted EBITDA of 90%+, outlook seems extremely bullish. Analysts are also bullish with revenue growth predictions to grow at 15% CAGR on a three-year window. The EPS estimates show a short term drop in 2025, but growth expectations are healthy from a three-year timeline perspective.

Risks

Risks of rising prices to the customer or lower discretionary spends could decouple the ecosystem lock-in. In such cases, stiff competition exists, particularly in mobility – a key driving segment for Uber. Driver base expansion (75% up between Jan 22 to Dec 24), which supported the growth leg, may also drop off as tariff pressures will impact them directly.

Long Term Bullish but Hold

In the short term i.e. 2025, a threat of a decrease in earnings is expected. This is backed by analyst estimates and will be due to higher indirect input costs (such as fuel, car maintenance impacting driver payout margin increases) and revenue drops in case of higher costs to customer or lower discretionary spending.

I believe, Uber will be able to wade through any incoming crisis without having to let go of the ecosystem lock-in, which is key. That should place Uber for decent long term (three-year) gains. Investors are likely to see share prices at lower levels in the short term. Better entry points can be found as valuation multiple as well as earnings will either stagnate or drag prices down. Hold Uber. Fresh investors wait for a better deal.

data.title: Fastenal: Expensive But Strong Growth Prospects
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I argue that Fastenal (NASDAQ:FAST) is set to rise to $99.6 by 2027 and commands a closer look. My investment thesis focuses on the shift away from the branch closure program, the focus on technology and digital services, secular tailwinds, and opportunity for a solid entry position.

The strategic shift away from branch consolidation helps improve customer captivity and is the foundation for helping move customers into higher revenue buckets. The focus on technology through FASTCrib is a growth catalyst for FAST's TAM, margin, and sales growth. Lastly, the current uncertainty, causing the entire market to drop, is opening opportunities to buy the expensive stock at an attractive enough discount.

My core argument is that the stock price is fairly valued given the current EPS, but is undervalued for the expectations of future earnings.

Company Background

Fastenal is one of the highest-quality businesses listed on any exchange. It commands a premium on its industry peers because of its economies of scale, coupled with customer captivity, service differentiation, and distinct culture. FAST is currently trading at a 1.5x P/e ratio compared to peer Grainger.

The company focuses on procurement management of essential tools, fasteners, and other supplies that are essential to their customers' supply chains. However, FAST is steadily shifting its narrative to become a comprehensive supply chain management partner, helping with everything from procurement to labour management and digital service provisions.

Supporting Arguments

Strategic Shift Toward Branch Expansion

Since 2018, FAST has been closing branches at a -5% CAGR. The main reason for closure was to pursue more meaningful clients, primarily those that are synergistic enough to open on-sites with. This has been a very successful strategy, as seen in the high customer retention rate and loyalty. Since there are far fewer costs and leaner teams, the onsite margins were a key feature to raise the American business to ~40% worth of sales, with the >$50k bucket increasing 7% as of Q1 2025.

However, the opportunity cost of this shift was quite evident as the customers worth <$10k fell by a CAGR of -1% since 2017. These customers make up 95% of Fastenal's customer base, accounting for 25% of their revenue. This has been a factor that has slowed down their revenue growth into the low single digits. However, the shift toward branch expansion unlocks a higher customer base, primarily <$5k customers like the rapidly growing SMEs, online-focused brands, etc.

This shift toward branch expansion also furthers the impact of Fastenal's focus on eCommerce services, as smaller key account customers that make up 25% of FAST revenue are often not on contracts. Hence, investments into FASTCrib and similar platforms, which help with automated order management, inventory insight, and a streamlined ordering experience, all provide additional revenue streams and higher margins that are baked into my model (shown in the valuation segment). This is also beneficial for the bucket with <$10k revenue customers because ~35% of industrial spending is unplanned for customers.

Moreover, since customers have increased bucket sizes up to ~50%, focusing on customer acquisition will fuel the double-digit revenue expansion Fastenal is reaching for. This move to double down on customer acquisition is further evidenced by Fastenal's customer solutions consultants and development specialists headcount increasing from 175 to ~300. Finally, Fastenal has been steadily expanding globally, with Asian branches increasing ~12% CAGR since 2018. The only fuel of growth is branches, since on-sites are primarily exclusive to North America. So, focusing on expanding branches will be essential to Fastenal's continued efforts to break into new markets and capture global customers that demand their services.

Lastly, Fastenal has streamlined the operations of its branches and moved to a more sales-based pay function for branch managers. This improves the SG&A costs of branches, allowing for the fixed costs to unlock the operating leverage needed to drive business growth. In fact, I expect SG&A as a % of sales to drop by 1%, accounting for the higher anticipated SG&A costs in 2025, given Trump uncertainties, as indicated by management in the recent call. Larger branches currently operate with a ~20% operating margin, but I do expect modest increases baked into the 3SM below. As they prototyped and tested different models for locations between East and West U.S., increasing the number of branches and changing their operations serves as an important case study needed to continue the global expansion without diseconomies of scale.

FASTCrib Investments

Fastenal, for most of its history, has been a procurement service provider for its customers. However, as digitization and AI are starting to penetrate each industry and take away previous competitive advantages, FAST is adapting to become more of a service provider for supply chain management + maintenance & work order management of different machines and units. 61% of sales were through their digital footprint (Q1 2025), which they are expecting to grow to 66-68% in October.

For a brief overview of FASTCrib, it is a program that serves the following needs and more for customers:

    • Certification of certain tools
    • Maintainance
    • Repairs
    • Data and visibility into all of the inventories/assets

This is key to unlocking a larger customer base and revenue stream alongside higher margins. Data-focused platforms and services are higher-margin businesses, which is needed for companies like Fastenal that have primary costs in COGS and other operating/fixed costs. Since Fastenal already performs similar tasks for its customers for customer supply-chain management, they have a growth opportunity to further increase switching costs and increase the stickiness of its revenues (fueled by FASTCrib subscriptions).

Moreover, they recently announced their partnership with Microsoft to leverage the change in customer preferences to have more data visibility for themselves by incorporating LLM models. This partnership is new for the industry and further boosts Fastenal's competitive advantage through differentiation for its customers.

Fastenal expects this program to increase 10x by 2027, moving from its current 200 customers to a 2000+ customer base. Whilst this sounds like a nearly impossible feat, the seamlessness for installation that requires no set-up costs and the improved customer satisfaction of current customers, like Safran's and Curtiss-Wright's experience, shows the value FASTCrib brings to the industry.

In fact, for highlighted customers, Safran and Curtiss-Wright, they have been able to take on a higher order backlog ever since FASTCrib was further pushed into Fastenal's offerings. Since 2022, both Safran and Curtiss-Wright were able to experience an 8.3% and 6.8% CAGR increase in order backlog. Moreover, since COVID-19, Safran's order backlog capabilities have increased 5.1% CAGR, showing the additional ability FASTCrib can provide when managing supply chains more effectively already.

Secular Tailwinds

It is also important to appreciate the secular tailwinds that have benefited (and continue to benefit) the industry. This is particularly emphasized with Fastenal as it has predominantly grown faster than the industry, and the street expects this trend to continue.

Hence, continued rise for data via data centres, energy via production plants, pharmaceuticals via pharma projects, etc., all continue to create the GDP+ growth Fastenal is expected to experience. Moreover, as Fastenal's customer base continues to expand into government and national contracts alongside institutions, evidenced by the double-digit government contract expansion for the last 12 consecutive months, Fastenal can benefit from the reshoring of manufacturing in most countries. With ongoing geopolitical uncertainty, arguments that manufacturing is moving closer to end markets rather than being concentrated in Asia are arising. Even though such near-term and volatile tariffs are ongoing, longer-term customer preferences may change, further creating tailwinds for Fastenal.

Valuation

To come up with my price target of $99.4, I used a two-fold approach of a P/E and a 3-statement model with an EPS schedule. I took a simple average of both, with the P/E ratio giving me a PT of $95.2 and the 3SM a PT of $103.5. I have added in my thinking below for more granularity.

Premium Discount

Fastenal trades at a 1.5x P/E compared to its closest competitor, Grainger (GWW). This is primarily why the consensus is at 'Hold' right now, and rightfully so.

Fastenal has a higher Net Income % by ~4%, creating a more attractive investment in terms of efficiency and potential growth. Moreover, FAST has a 1.5% higher dividend yield, returning more to shareholders. Market expectations are also far higher, with EPS estimates ~15% higher than GWW for Q1 2025. Lastly, the company has a far stronger culture. To quantify this, I compared the difference in revenue/employee and SG&A/employee, leading to a 12% higher employee efficiency brought in by the Late Bob Kierlin's unique culture.

Since these contributions demand only a 32.5% premium, the 50% premium is higher than the fair value given Fastenal's current earnings. Assuming a 32.5% premium to Grainger, I believe a P/e ratio of 34.2x is justified.

Applying a 34.2x P/E to the current estimates of a $2.2 EPS, I believe an entry price of under ~$75 is much more attractive than the ~$80/share at the time of writing this article. This is because I believe it is a fair price for the current earnings in 2025. However, applying the same P/E to the 2027 EPS estimates of $2.8 gives a PT of $95.2.

3 Statement Model

For the second half of my valuation, I used a 3-statement model as follows:

Starting with the Income Statement:

I anticipate revenue growth to rise to the high single-digits based on the revenue build below. This is because I factor in a 2% annual growth in the number of branches in line with the first thesis. I assume the on-site expansion will continue its ~10% growth, as it has historically given no specific guidance by management. However, in terms of average monthly revenue, I expect branch averages to increase ~3% and onsite ~2% primarily due to additional revenue streams with FASTCrib, increased bargaining power through Fastenal's expansion, and a more sophisticated supply chain management ecosystem. It must be noted that I factored in the incremental pricing strategy for 2025 as per management's guidance, contributing to the heightened revenue growth in 2025 compared to 2024.

As for the margins, I expect a marginal change in gross profit for 2025 before moderate increases due to increased uncertainty with the Trump administration. The moderate increase stems from the increased sales from the digital footprint, as noted in Thesis 2. For SG&A, I expect a slight increase in 2025 costs as indicated in the Q1 call, before efficiency kicks in, resulting from the strategic shift mentioned in Thesis 1.

For the balance sheet:

The balance sheet reflects the political uncertainty and long-term objectives of Fastenal. I expect cash to deplete quite significantly because of the need for higher inventories, increasing working capital, and higher CAPEX. However, I expect cash to fluctuate as Fastenal focuses on further streamlining its own supply chain, as it did post-COVID-19, and continues to increase CAPEX toward branch expansion, which is also shown in the growth in PP&E.

Cash Flow Statement:

I expect changes to the working capital to be quite volatile as FAST continues to navigate such uncertain and unexpected conditions. However, the rest of the line items are clear in terms of Fastenal's strategic shift in certain areas.

For further granularity, I have attached below the schedules created for Working Capital, Retained Earnings, Interest Income/Expense, and EPS:

The most notable points are temporary increases in DSO for 2025 and 2026 stemming from the increased uncertainty, which will likely create delays in payments from customers. For inventory turns, I simply averaged them over the prior 3 years, accounting for the cyclicality and uncertainty as Fastenal navigated the post-COVID situation.

I expect D&A as a % of CAPEX to initially decrease as Fastenal focuses on opening new branches, investing in new infrastructure, and technologies. However, the metric gradually increases, accounting for the D&A related to the branch openings.

As for dividends, I expect the payout ratio to increase based on the increased dividends announcement to $0.44 in the Q1 earnings call. This flows into my EPS schedule as I account for Net Income, Share Repurchases, and Dividends. I expect the share price to grow in line with the growth of Net Income, anticipating an EPS of ~$2.8 through 2027. This method gave me a PT of $103.

Peer Analysis

In terms of peer analysis, compared to the overall industry, Fastenal is at quite a premium. However, this is because Fastenal provides a unique service that doesn't have parallel competition. Grainger, the closest competitor, is the most accurate comparison out of the comp set because of the g/s offered. Compared to Grainger, Fastenal trades at a premium, as mentioned above, for the respective reasons. As for EV/EBTIDA, Fastenal trades at a 1.5x, albeit they are more efficient when looking at the ROIC difference of ~2.5% in favour of GWW.

Risks

The primary current risk, as for many companies today, comes from the Trump administration. Such quick tariffs do not change investment projects, primarily because they could be lifted tomorrow, making no economic sense for certain projects. Yet, Fastenal has to focus on its supply chain and its ability to absorb the higher costs without negatively harming the business from the top and bottom line. They have focused on doing so through incremental price hikes for their customers, which may need to be altered depending on how the economic climate changes. Aside from this, Fastenal has the opportunity to create even stickier revenues as they show customers their capabilities to reduce price volatility. Fastenal is the top 5 consumer for all of their suppliers, creating bargaining power to reduce further supply chain compromises that competitors face. Moreover, their ability to work directly with manufacturers creates increased visibility, which is priceless for customers dealing with the geopolitical turmoil unfolding.

Overall

Fastenal is an expensive stock, as shown in the valuation segment, yet future prospects label it as a buy. I believe a good entry point lies 5-6% below current prices, creating the opportunity for a longer-term hold. Fastenal's strategic and tactical moves to open more branches, increase its digital footprint through FASTCrib and partnerships, and core secular tailwinds all make for a good investment opportunity for the investor interested in more expensive stocks.

data.title: Trade Wars And The U.S. Dollar
data.description:
Rapid U.S. policy changes pose challenges for investors accustomed to a global financial system anchored in U.S. markets and assets.

As U.S. trade policy transforms, investors are grappling with the implications for the world’s preeminent reserve currency.

Announced U.S. tariffs aimed at revitalizing domestic manufacturing in the long run could first weaken the U.S. economy and rekindle inflation, casting a shadow over the U.S. investment outlook. As trade barriers rise, longstanding partners are reconsidering their economic ties with the U.S. and contemplating alternative alliances.

With its protectionist policy pivots, the U.S. is giving investors worldwide an occasion to rethink long-held assumptions about the U.S. investment landscape. Witness the recent parallel slides of the U.S. dollar, U.S. equities, and U.S. Treasuries - a combination more often associated with emerging market (EM) economies.

The U.S. has long enjoyed a privileged position, with the dollar serving as the global reserve currency and Treasuries as the go-to reserve asset. However, this status is not guaranteed. If global capital flows into U.S. assets dwindle, it could point toward a more multipolar world with a diminished reliance on a singular reserve currency.

Paradigm shift

For decades, the U.S. has operated on a model of consumption, importing more than it exports. The dynamic of U.S. consumers satiating themselves on an endless supply of cheap foreign goods, and the resulting current account deficit, creates a U.S. capital account surplus.

Nations in the post-Cold-War era - including allies bolstered by NATO security assurances - have been able to prioritize savings and investment over national security spending, often funneling dollars into American financial assets. These international capital inflows have bolstered the dollar’s reserve status, a cornerstone of U.S. economic exceptionalism.

With tariffs disrupting this balance, the financing of America’s twin current account and fiscal deficits may become more challenging, unless there is fiscal support, as countries pursue greater economic and military self-reliance. The breakdown of longstanding global correlations could be painful for a global investor, who may be left wondering how many U.S. assets to own.

A diminished policy safety net

The U.S. enters this period with historically high sovereign debt levels and inflation exceeding the Federal Reserve’s 2% target. If U.S. companies are compelled to manufacture goods domestically and shift supply chains away from China, production costs will rise, resulting in diminished productivity and higher prices.

The Fed will have to chart an interest rate path that balances resurgent inflation expectations with dimming U.S. growth projections. Conversely, currency appreciation in other regions may alleviate inflationary concerns, allowing the Bank of Japan and the European Central Bank to adopt a more dovish stance.

Investors have come to expect forceful government intervention during economic and market downturns. Today, U.S. fiscal support appears less likely - not by choice, but due to limited capacity for additional debt. This era of strained geopolitical relations could mean far less global policy coordination than during previous crises.

Investment implications

U.S. markets appear to be mirroring the dynamics of the U.K. and EM, characterized by steeper yield curves, a weaker currency, and a structural risk premium - higher compensation for holding a country’s assets. A self-inflicted supply-side shock, similar to Brexit, has led to a stagflationary outlook. Lessons from the U.K. suggest that the U.S. economy may need to rebalance toward structurally lower growth and higher inflation in the absence of fiscal capacity.

Having long benefited in many ways from the existing ecosystem, the U.S. may face hurdles as the global order evolves. For investors, the return of equity may soon take precedence over the return on equity, prompting a shift toward diversification and a stronger home country bias.

Based on this outlook, here are some investment strategies:

  • Underweight the U.S. dollar: The U.S. has the largest negative net international investment position (NIIP), financed by global capital. As this rebalances, the dollar may weaken.
  • Overweight global duration (Europe, EM, Japan, U.K.): Duration – a gauge of interest-rate sensitivity that is typically higher for longer-dated bonds – is challenging to forecast in the U.S., but alternatives appear attractive (for more, see our April 2025 Cyclical Outlook, “Seeking Stability”).
  • Favor trades that benefit from yield curve steepening: The shift from a global focus on economic efficiency to one emphasizing nationalism introduces a greater fiscal risk premium.
  • Underweight credit: We expect a more significant divergence between investment-grade (IG) and high yield (HY) credit, as IG balance sheets remain more flexible and insurance companies continue to support IG credit, a trend unlikely to extend to HY.

In a more multipolar world, there may no longer be a need for a singular reserve currency. Multiple options would be necessary to safeguard national security interests, ensure diversification, and deliver stable returns.

Thus far, this is a self-inflicted wound for the U.S. The dollar has only recently begun to structurally weaken. Sentiment and market performance could quickly reverse if we see a shift toward less disruptive - and more predictable - U.S. trade policies.

Disclosures

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic and industry conditions.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the manager and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. data.title: Ubisoft: The Tencent Deal Could Spark A Game-Changing Comeback
data.description:
Ubisoft (OTCPK:UBSFY) dropped about 30% since the March 27th announcement of a $1.25B Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) deal that, if successful, could mark a consequential bounce back for the beaten gaming stock, which lost over 85% of its value over the last five years.

Investment Thesis

We believe most Ubisoft investors are fixated on project delays, canceled titles, and a potentially troublesome balance sheet. In doing so, they are missing what could be the biggest opportunity for UBSFY over the last decade.

This is due to Tencent's $1.25B non-controlling investment for a 25% stake into a newly formed Ubisoft subsidiary with its most valuable IP (Assassin's Creed, Rainbow Six and Far Cry).

If the deal closes successfully, Ubisoft's financial position is immediately improved and any medium-term liquidity concern is tackled, while supporting Ubisoft's presence in the mobile gaming market, as well as the biggest gaming region in the world, Asia.

The company is going through an encouraging restructuring process, including ahead-of-schedule cost-cutting measures and termination of underperforming titles like xDefiant. Positive early results on highly anticipated releases like Assassin's Creed Shadows and a strong mobile game pipeline developed in partnership with Tencent, could boost bookings growth and restore investor confidence.

Finally, our sum-of-parts multiple valuation demonstrates significant upside (+176%), which we believe makes UBSFY a deeply mispriced turnaround story, assuming a successful deal closure by the end of 2025.

Company Overview

Company Description and Revenue Streams

Ubisoft is a leading video game company with prominent titles like Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six, Assassin's Creed, and Far Cry. They primarily focus on consoles (56% of Net Bookings, FY24) and PC (32%), with plans to expand further into mobile (6%).

Rather than targeting one-time sales, Ubisoft aims to retain its user base with high-quality, enduring games and monetize using online transactions for cosmetic items or game progression. For FY24, Player Recurring Investment stood at ~38% of Net Bookings, and Back-Catalog Net Bookings amounted to 65% of Net Bookings.

The company is moving towards the higher margin fully digital distribution, but still sells physical copies of its games (14% of Revenue). Ubisoft also licenses major brands (e.g. Star Wars) and possesses 15-year cloud streaming rights for Activision Blizzard titles like Call of Duty (now part of MSFT).

Finally, Ubisoft's strategic focus and investments target two core verticals: open world adventures (e.g. AC Shadows, SW Outlaws), a €25B market with high barriers of entry, and Game-as-a-Service (e.g. Rainbow Six Siege), a €120B market according to the company.

Weak Financial Performance Post-Pandemic, With Signs Of Rebounding

Similar to most gaming companies, Ubisoft saw a temporary boost at its top and bottom lines during 2020-2022, driven by the stay-at-home COVID-19 mandates and stimulus measures, as gamers spent their disposable income on Ubisoft titles.

Thereafter, the company saw its revenue drop from -18% from 2021 to €1,814M in 2023, while margins turned negative. On a positive note, Ubisoft's financials rebounded somewhat in 2024, exceeding its peak 2021 revenue by about +8%, and margins saw healthy improvement to 22%, 14%, and 7% FCF, Operating, and Net Income margins, respectively.

Following the failure of xDefiant (to be discontinued in June 2025) and underperformance of Star Wars Outlaws, the company's short-term financial performance relies primarily on the success of Assassin's Creed Shadows (launched March 2025), as well as the performance of its established titles including Rainbow Six Siege, The Crew and Far Cry 5.

Financial Position Hinges On Tencent Deal

Ubisoft has €933M in cash and over €2.4B in debt (half due by 2027).

Its debt distribution does not appear problematic in the short term, with €325M due in 2025. However, the ~€1 billion due by 2027 could become an issue if the deal with Tencent falls apart due to the anticipated €1.16B ($1.25B) cash injection into the company as part of the deal, part of which will be used for debt extinguishment.

Notable Game Portfolio

Ubisoft boasts a strong franchise portfolio with several games performing well over multiple years, particularly the Assassin's Creed and Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six franchises.

The deal with Tencent highlights the strength and long-term potential for the company's IP, which appears to be discounted by current market prices (see "Valuation").

Why The Deal with Tencent Changes Everything

Reduced Operational and Executional Risks

With 8 games in development for the next 3 years, the partnership with the biggest and most experienced game publishing company in the world reduces the probability of delays and flops.

While Ubisoft lags behind on mobile gaming, upcoming titles like Resurgence, Rainbow Six Mobile, and AC Jade aim to tap the mobile gaming industry, potentially putting their brand on the map.

Table 2: Ubisoft games in development (Ubisoft).

No. Game Platform Launch Date
1 Tom Clancy's The Division Resurgence iOS, Android TBD
2 Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Mobile iOS, Android TBD
3 Assassin's Creed Codename Jade iOS, Android TBD
4 Assassin's Creed Codename Hexe/ Neo TBD TBD
5 Beyond Good and Evil TBD 6/25/2025
6 Prince of Persia Remake TBD TBD
7 Project U Splinter Cell Remake TBD TBD
8 Source: Company Announcements, Bloomberg Intelligence, TBD TBD

Improved Financial Outlook

Assuming a successful closing of the Tencent deal by the end of 2025, Ubisoft will find itself in a considerably healthier financial position.

Taking into account the $1.25B cash injection from Tencent (about 80% of its Net Debt), the current net debt to equity at 83% will plunge to a healthy 17%. This provides Ubisoft with a significant breathing room and the ability to invest in long-term growth.

The successful launch of Ubisoft's highly anticipated AC Shadows is also a positive sign, which supports the Bookings numbers for 2025.

The company guides for breakeven FCF in FY25 (est. EUR -33M) and targeting €171M in FY26.

Potential of Tapping New Revenue Streams in Asia and Mobile Markets

Due to Tencent's inherent experience in the Asian market and role as the biggest game publisher in the world with multiple flagship titles (e.g. Honor of Kings, PUBG Mobile and League of Legends), we believe Ubisoft's top and new titles might experience a TAM (Total Addressable Market) expansion due to Tencent's foothold in the Asian and mobile gaming market.

Table 3: Top countries and markets by video game revenues in 2024 (Source: Newzoo).

Ranking

 Market Revenue (USD) Player Count

1.

 China $47.0B 701.6M

2.

 United States $46.1B 220.5M

3.

 Japan $16.3B 74.1M

4.

 South Korea $7.5B 33.9M

5.

 Germany $6.1B 52.1M

Table 3 above demonstrates the considerable potential for Ubisoft to tap the Asian market, particularly China, which according to Newzoo, is the biggest gaming market in the world as of 2024.

Complementing the figures above, Statista reported ~$88B in revenues from Asia Pacific in 2022, while North America (including the US and Canada) accounted for around half of that, at $48B, demonstrating the huge potential of tapping the Asia Pacific market.

Finally, mobile gaming is more common in Asia, with a penetration of about 90% (vs. 70% in the US). The potential for successful mobile game launches that appeal to the Asian audience, areas where Tencent is experienced, could boost Ubisoft's bookings beyond current projections.

Favorable Conditions for Deal Clearance

We believe the acquisition is unlikely to face backlash from regulators due to Tencent's non-controlling 25% stake, which preserves Ubisoft's operational autonomy without triggering foreign acquisition regulations.

Also, Tencent's existing ~10% stake in Ubisoft means that its incentives are aligned with Ubisoft's and its shareholders', all of which would benefit from a higher valuation on UBSFY. This likely explains the favorable terms on the subsidiary deal and justifies our argument about the high likelihood of deal closure.

Ubisoft's Downfall and How It Can Be Saved

Three Painful Years for Investors

UBSFY stock has suffered multiple blows since late 2021. Using the stock Price and adjusted EPS 2025 and 2026 expectations graph, we differentiate market-driven selloffs with company-specific developments driven by fundamentals.

The company experienced two significant re-ratings over the last three years.

  1. January 2023 (Revisions Announcement): revised FY23 guidance, with Net Bookings expected to decline by at least -10% (prev. expected to grow +10%) and non-IFRS Operating Income at EUR -500M driven by weak game performance, postponed releases and a EUR 500M R&D write-down.
  2. October 2024 (2FQ25 Earnings): Net Bookings missed analyst estimates, landing at the low end of already lowered guidance. Ubisoft guided for significantly weaker holiday quarter YoY, driven by production delays, weak releases and postponement of AC Shadows past the holiday window.

Persistent Multiple Compression

Examining Ubisoft's Blended Forward EV/EBITDA graph demonstrates the consistent multiple compression over the last four years, resulting in an attractive 2.9x BF EV/EBITDA, given the turnaround potential following the Tencent deal announcement (see "Why The Deal with Tencent Changes Everything").

From a top-down perspective, the persistent downfall since 2021 is attributed to several factors:

  1. Multiple project delays and poor performance on launch, including xDefiant and Star Wars Outlaws, are causing highly volatile Bookings and earnings growth metrics.
  2. Consumer backlash due to aggressive monetization practices, including paywalled content and expensive premium editions.
  3. Political controversy for some of its recent titles, including AC: Shadows and Star Wars Outlaws.
  4. Unfavorable ownership structure due to the Guillemot family and Tencent stake, which hinders the prospects for a potential buyout.
  5. Low presence in mobile gaming.

Going forward, we believe that factors (1), (4) and (5) will be improved considerably following the deal with Tencent (discussed in "Why The Deal with Tencent Changes Everything"), while (2) and (3) can be fixed with successful game lunches considering past missteps.

Depressed Valuation Compared To Peers

Ubisoft stock trades at a substantial discount to its peers, particularly when looking at the BF (Blended Forward) EV/Rev, EV/EBITDA, and P/E multiples.

Due to Ubisoft's considerable depreciated upfront game development expenses, EV/EBIT appears closer to industry averages (BF P/E 12.4x vs 14.0x); however, the company trades at a -38% (BF P/E 11.8x vs 19.0x) forward bottom line discount, demonstrating ample room for multiple expansion.

CD Projekt (OTCPK:OTGLF), a company that faced similar issues to Ubisoft, including project delays and consumer backlash, is trading at premium valuations compared to Ubisoft.

Encouraging Restructuring and Cost-Cutting Progress

Ubisoft initiated cost-cutting measures, including headcount reductions, following post-pandemic development disruptions. Management highlighted their focus on a “player-centric” turnaround strategy centered on improving game quality and customer satisfaction.

In its latest 3FQ25 earnings release, the company announced that it expects to exceed €200 million in fixed cost reductions by FY2024 - 25, ahead of its original schedule.

The savings are driven by a stringent investment policy, discontinuation of underperforming games like XDefiant, and the closure and restructuring of several studios with a focus on high-cost regions.

Furthermore, Yves Guillemot, Ubisoft's Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer, mentioned that these efforts are expected to continue into FY26, aiming to surpass the initial cost-cutting targets by a considerable margin.

We believe that the decisive termination of underperforming titles and delivery of ahead-of-schedule savings, along with the commitment to follow through with further such measures, is a positive development and indication that Ubisoft is on the right path for a turnaround.

With a lighter, more agile business structure, Ubisoft will be able to focus its efforts on what matters: delivering high-quality gaming content to gamers on time in a profitable manner.

Mobile Gaming Growth Is Key to Ubisoft's Turnaround

The company's future growth relies on successful Mobile games, the biggest gaming market in the world according to Statista. The growth in mobile is expected to continue, with an impressive 11.8% CAGR reaching $438B by 2030.

Mobile is also the dominant gaming medium according to Newzoo, with $92.6B in annual revenues. Note that this statistic differs considerably from direct Statista figures (~$197B). What is clear, however, is mobile's dominance over Console ($56.1B) and PC ($37.1B), as well as the huge total addressable market in which Ubisoft operates.

Valuation

Our valuation uses a sum-of-parts multiple valuation approach using data provided by Ubisoft on the deal with Tencent. According to the excerpt from the company's announcement, we get the following data:

  • Pre-money Subsidiary EV = €4,000M ($4,377M)
  • Tencent Investment = €1,160M ($1,250M)
  • EV/Sales (FY23-FY25E) = 4x

Using the EV/Sales multiple of 4x and the FY23-FY25E Sales of €2,002M ($2,191M), the Sales attributed to the subsidiary and parent company (remaining IP portfolio) amount to €1,000M ($1,093M) and €1,002 ($1,095M) respectively.

Sum-of-Parts EV/Adj. EBITDA Valuation

For our EV/Adj EBITDA valuation, in the absence of franchise-specific financial metrics, we attribute 70% of Ubisoft's Adj EBITDA to the IP of the newly formed subsidiary. Our estimation reflects the fact that the new subsidiary includes Ubisoft's top-performing franchises, which likely enjoy greater margins.

This is also reflected in our target multiples of 7.0x (subsidiary) and 3.5x (parent), which are based on industry averages. According to Seeking Alpha, the sector median forward EV/EBITDA amounts to 7.74x. We believe the newly formed subsidiary with strong IP and pipeline demands multiples near industry averages at a minimum.

Sum-of-Parts EV/Sales Valuation

Using a similar approach, we use Seeking Alpha sector medians to anchor our multiple targets. For the subsidiary, we set the EV/Sales multiple to 1.8x, slightly below the industry median of 1.83x. For the parent company, we set the target multiple to 1.0x to reflect a slower growth and lower profitability of the remaining game portfolio.

Blended Target Price

Our equal-weighting approach reflects our caution around limitations such as limited segment EBITDA disclosure and the risk of over-allocating value to high-performing franchises might cause fair price overestimation.

By combining the EV/Sales and EV/Adj. EBITDA target prices, we arrive at a Blended Target Price of $5.40, reflecting a +176% upside from current prices ($1.96).

Target Price Weighting
EV/Adj EBITDA $6.82 50%
EV/Sales $3.97 50%
Blended Target Price $5.40 100%

Action Recommendation

Taking into account the significant upside potential (+176%) and the high certainty of the deal closure, we initiate UBSFY with a 'BUY' rating.

Risks

As evidenced by the significant share price depreciation over the last years, Ubisoft stock exhibits risks that potential investors should be aware of. Below, we list the most impactful risks for the stock in the foreseeable future.

First, Ubisoft is expected to deliver 30-40% bookings growth in the Mobile segment for FY25-FY27. Failure to meet these targets could pressure the stock further.

Second, Ubisoft failed to achieve or exceed its guidance numerous times, which can be repeated. For example, in 2FQ25, management guided for €380M in 3FQ25 Net Bookings, after which it reiterated its guidance to €300M, a -21% downward revision.

Finally, the deal with Tencent is expected to close by the end of 2025. There is a non-insignificant probability that the deal falls apart due to European regulator pushback or unforeseen circumstances by either party. This would leave Ubisoft exposed to the incoming debt maturities with unfavorable liquidity, particularly in 2027.

Conclusion

Ubisoft's multi-year slump appears to present investors with an appealing opportunity. The $1.25B Tencent deal not only alleviates short to medium-term financial pressures but also positions Ubisoft to benefit from long-term growth, particularly in mobile gaming and Asian markets, both of which are some of the gaming industry's biggest submarkets.

The aggressive and successful cost-cutting measures, along with a more intentional game portfolio and early signs of success from Assassin's Creed Shadows, Ubisoft appears to be on the path to a meaningful turnaround.

While execution risks remain around mobile bookings and deal closure with Tencent, the upside potential cannot be ignored. Our sum-of-parts valuation suggests a +176% upside with relatively conservative target multiples, based on industry averages.

Given the improving fundamentals, enhanced strategic positioning and strong alignment between Tencent and Ubisoft's long-term interests, we initiate UBSFY with a 'BUY' rating.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which ran through April 17. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in future competitions, click here to find out more.

data.title: Palantir Isn't What You Think: Revealing The Growth Driver Markets Are Missing
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Inversia Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) is not a traditional SaaS company, it is creating the operating system for enterprise AI. With its Ontology layer, Palantir converts business logic to machine-readable code, allowing AI agents to act directly in actual-world workflows. As inference prices decrease and performance converges, value will reside in deployment infrastructure, not in the creators of models. Palantir is already capitalizing upon this shift with hyperscaler-like 44% FCF margins and 64% YoY U.S. commercial revenue growth. Regardless of this fact, however, the stock is still mispriced by traditional valuation models. Based upon execution speed, protocol-level entrenchment, and free cash flow possibilities in the long-run, I assign Palantir a Strong Buy rating with a price target of ~$175/share.

Underestimated Potential

Consensus treats Palantir as a legacy government contractor with scattered commercial ambitions. Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform (AIP) is fast becoming the only operational interface that commoditized LLMs can plug into for enterprise-scale value creation. In a world where inference costs are plummeting and model performance is converging, the next AI winners will be defined not by model superiority but by deployment infrastructure. Palantir has spent two decades building exactly that.

Palantir's growth engine is also essentially a self-reinforcing cycle. Ontology translates business thought processes into AI-executable processes. The more companies use AIP to make automation-driven decisions, such as underwriting, maintenance, or scheduling, the smarter and more embedded in the organization the system becomes with each deployment. Instead of a standard SaaS growth model, this cycle feeds upon itself creating a flywheel effect, influencing both scale and margin growth.

The market still does not fully understand the protocol-layer operator that is becoming the autonomous enterprise's operating system. This analysis aims to demonstrate, via triangulation, that Palantir is still cheap as it is not a software bet but a call option on the AI labor economy itself.

Palantir's Ontology Is the Real AI OS

Palantir's greatest asset is least known: its Ontology. Two years ago, the company staked its position on the fact that LLMs would converge, commoditize, and fold on cost. Q4 2024 made that thesis a reality with the advent of DeepSeek R1. The rest of the market pursued frontier models.

Palantir wagered that the AI winner would be the orchestrator, not the generator. Ontology is that orchestrator, transforming enterprise logic into machine-readable blueprints that AI agents can execute. AIP isn't like chat-based copilots stuck in sandboxed demos. It runs at full enterprise scale. It's the difference between enabling conversation and enabling transformation.

AIP is in production across industries, with measurable output. A five-day back-end process at a Fortune 500 bank was trimmed to a three-minute process. An auto parts supplier took a 100-hour engineering validation loop down to a process lasting less than an hour with AI labor. ER wait times at Cleveland Clinic fell by 38 minutes and OR inefficiency by 40% at Cleveland Clinic. None of these outcomes resulted from new models. They resulted from workflow integration enabled by Ontology.

Repeating this, a competitor would require full-stack visibility into structured and unstructured enterprise data, deep vertical implementation heritage, and a productized abstraction layer derived from that heritage. That's a decade if only such data access were legally possible. This is why Palantir's acceleration isn't just sticky; it's nearly unbeatable. Ontology is the bridge over the LLM deployment gap. The implication: Palantir owns the runtime layer of enterprise AI, and the market has no framework to value it properly.

In simple words, Palantir's Ontology is an organization's digital brain operating in real-time. It not only keeps track of information but also organizes decision-making. Rather than an inert warehouse of information, Ontology ties people, machines, and AI together with a single language of "objects" such as machines, plants, and reports.

Each decision is dissected into three components: facts (information), rules (logic), and actions (outcomes). This enables Palantir's platform to automate workflows, suggest next steps, and function as a layer of control, not a dashboard. Whether it's an executive tailoring supply chain plans or a technician employing AI to detect defects on a production floor, every interaction is influenced by Ontology's knowledge of how the enterprise works.

Its power exists in tying human inputs, AI reasoning, and real-world effects together in a single cohesive feedback loop. It turns the platform into decision-making's operational runtime, enabling scalable AI-human collaboration that goes past dashboards to enterprise-level autonomy in real time.

The SBC Mirage Breaks: Palantir's Leverage is Real

The most apparent divergence in Palantir's financials is in perception vs. operational leverage. Long critics claim that the growth at Palantir is subsidized by stock-based comp (SBC), but Q4 2024 saw a categorical departure. Even with a significant decrease in outstanding total options (from 278 million to 178 million), the total intrinsic value of options ballooned from $2.4 billion to $11.8 billion. This points to concentrated value creation in fewer, richer-granted options, more performance-based than issuances en masse.

More significant still, Palantir implemented Stock Appreciation Rights (SARs) in 2024, awarding 51.6 million units with a $50.73 exercise price, which was well in excess of historical standards. Just 6.4 million SARs stood outstanding at year's end, with an overall intrinsic value of just $128 million. This verifies that Q4's accelerated vesting-related expense of $131 million was an isolated occurrence and not part of a dilutive engine.

In aggregate, vested and exercisable stock options represent only 80.1 million shares with a weighted average exercise price of $6.66 that are still deeply in-the-money but are probably already included in diluted shares outstanding of 2.45 billion.

From Defense Prime to AI Industrial Policy Platform

Investors tend to categorize Palantir as a defense contractor. However, that shortened assessment downplays how deeply it is becoming part of America's national industrial policy. Its evolution from battlefield software to manufacturing protocol is becoming self-evident through initiatives such as Warp Speed, a contemporary industrial OS used by Anduril and L3Harris. Palantir has significantly compressed go-to-market times for partners with FedRAMP High clearance, transforming the federal software procurement obstacle course into a frictionless onboarding motion.

Q4 was a turning point for federal positioning. Revenues from the U.S. government were 45% higher YoY as Mission Manager was deployed in Special Ops and CJADC2 rolled out across Spacecom and Stratcom. Outside of defense, NHS deployment of Palantir in the UK hit 87 hospital trusts and 28 integrated care boards. But the dominant story is domestic. In the face of growing geopolitical tension along with the advent of Chinese models such as DeepSeek-R1, Palantir is a de facto AI national security vendor. CTO Shyam Sankar's Senate testimony set the AI arms race in existential terms, and why Palantir is winning contracts because it can translate from R&D into operations in weeks, not years.

This position as infrastructure for a sovereign AI stack provides Palantir with distinct defensibility. It's not commercial AI vendors pursuing EBITDA by charging consumption prices. Palantir is being interwoven into national workflows, defense, energy, logistics, and manufacturing. These are not contracts. They're dependencies. And during an election year dominated by calls for reshoring, industrial revitalization, and digital sovereignty, Palantir's dual-commercial-and-government business model is perfectly in tune with the zeitgeist.

Moreover, what makes the flywheel even more resilient is its capital-light. Even with $803 million worth of U.S. commercial TCV bookings in Q4, which grew 134% YoY, headcount expansion is kept in check. There's no salesforce race. Rather, Palantir is sustained by product-driven expansion through use-case virality. U.S. commercial customer count rose 73% YoY to 382, with in-seat remaining deal value in the segment rose 99%. The same customers are now contributing $65 million on average on the top 20 accounts, which is 18% growth YoY.

Additionally, the gross margin remains at 83% for 2024. No heavy CapEx headwind, property and equipment acquisition was a trivial $12 million in FY24. Accrual quality is strong, with growth in deferred revenue and billings ($778 million in Q4) tracking with growth in TCV, reducing distortions in the timing of charges. Note that Palantir is not massaging metrics by making aggressive non-GAAP reconciliations. Even excluding SARs, EPS was $0.07 GAAP, $0.14 on a base-adjusted basis, both reconcilable to cash performance at the core.

The Fragility Beneath the Flywheel: Valuation, Geography, and Execution Risk

Even with Palantir's increasing dominance of enterprise AI OS, there are a number of risks that might stall its momentum. Firstly, the heavy dependence of the company on US commercial and government demand exposes it to a geographical concentration risk. While momentum in the US is still phenomenal, with US commercial growth up 54% in 2024 coupled with defense programs such as Maven and TITAN growth, foreign commercial growth is sluggish at single-digit rates. This unevenness opens Palantir up to policy shifts at home, procurement cycles, or policy bottlenecks. Additionally, close integration with US defense could lead to political blowback or resistance in growth across regions such as Europe or Asia, with stronger concerns around data sovereignty.

Second, there is substantial operating leverage evident in Palantir's AIP stack, but not especially in valuation optics or long-term overhang from stock-based comp. At $90+, it's 158x forward EV and over 50x forward sales, levels that require near-perfect execution. While the company is moving in the direction of performance-linked stock awards, 2.45 billion diluted shares mute per-share value accretion unless FCF growth runs much ahead of dilution.

Even if not, if countries with non-negotiable capital markets suddenly wake up to a maturation of their own locally hosted AI infrastructures, moat perception could erode. Execution risk for high-drawdown, high-ticket contracts also exists, especially in highly regulated verticals such as health care and defense where procurement slowdowns or contract renegotiations could hurt both topline and investor sentiment.

Palantir's Real Multiple Is Infrastructure: Rethinking Valuation from the Protocol Layer Up

Palantir is a high-growth AI consulting company in terms of trading profile. Operationally, though, it operates like an infrastructural vertical stack. Standard multiple metrics (trailing P/E, EV/EBITDA) obscure what drives the underlying economics. To measure asymmetry, I use two models: adjusted FCF-based DCF (discounting future monetization quality) and normalized ARR multiple comparisons (comparing flywheel efficiency with that of comparable SaaS).

Palantir's long-term value needs to be reassessed not by such standard SaaS metrics as EV/Sales or trailing P/E but in terms of enterprise infrastructure equivalent to an operating system, not a subscription application. Its financials are already starting to show that shift: in 2024, it generated $1.25 billion in free cash flow with a 44% margin, with U.S. commercial revenue increasing 54% from last year.

DCF Based on FCF Parameters:

  • Initial Free Cash Flow (2025): $1.6 billion This captures Palantir's underlying run-rate derived from FY2024 performance and is consistent with management's inferred guidance with respect to accelerating U.S. commercial growth.
  • FCF CAGR (2025-2030): 40%: It assumes ongoing growth in U.S. commercial, better monetization of original contracts (120% NDR), and increased use of AIP agents across industries.
  • Growth Rate after 2030: 7% It is above a standard GDP-aligned terminal rate thanks to Palantir's structural moat, protocol-level stickiness, and increasing dominance on mission-critical decision-making. It is also supported by its emerging position as a protocol-layer AI platform where Ontology-based architecture deeply integrates into enterprise and government processes, inducing nearly permanent vendor lock-in and increasing monetization over time. Palantir isn't selling software in the way traditional software companies have sold tools in the past; rather, it is empowering decision infrastructure, and as decision-making agents become ubiquitous in industries, its value capture increases in tandem with the digital economy itself, not merely IT budgets.
  • WACC (Discount Rate): 8.5% This is a function of Palantir's distinctive capital structure, no debt, more than $5.2 billion of cash, and high margins. While 8% WACC may be applied by traditional SaaS peers, I assign 8.5% due to higher equity risk (beta), geopolitical dependencies (revenue from the U.S. government), and long-term nature of Palantir's flywheel. This offsets near-term volatility with long-term defensive qualities.

Palantir's FCF Engine Justifies a ~$427 Billion Valuation

Assuming 40% annual growth from a starting value of a base year FCF of $1.6 billion from 2025, the following FCF path is generated (in billions):

  • 2026 = $2.24,
  • 2027 = $3.14,
  • 2028 = $4.39,
  • 2029 = $6.15,
  • and 2030 = $8.61 billion.

These are then discounted back to present value backed by a WACC of 8.5% using standard discount factors: 0.922 (2026), 0.849 (2027), 0.783 (2028), 0.722 (2029), and 0.665 (2030). The respective present values for each year (in billions: $2.06, $2.66, $3.44, $4.44, and $5.72). The total value of all discounted cash flows before 2030 is $18.3 billion.

Then, the terminal value is calculated by employing the Gordon Growth Model: TV = 2030 FCF × (1 + g) / (WACC - g) = $8.61 billion × 1.07 / (0.085 - 0.07) = $614.38 billion. Discounting back this terminal value to 2025 at 8.5% for 5 years would provide a present value of: $614.38 billion × 0.665 = $408.25 billion.

Adding the discounted interim cash flows ($18.3 billion) and discounted terminal value ($408.25 billion) produces an EV of $426.55 billion. Adding an additional $5.2 billion of net cash (as of Palantir's Q4 2024 balance sheet) provides an Equity Value of $431.75 billion. Then, dividing by 2.475 billion diluted shares (adjusting for probable option and SAR dilution) produces an intrinsic value per share of around ~$175/share.

Reducing Palantir's terminal growth rate from 7% to a more conservative 4% cuts its intrinsic value sharply, from $174/share down to around $63/share. This dramatic decrease highlights how sensitive DCF models are to long-term growth projections, especially for companies such as Palantir, whose value is created mostly many years out. The EV under the 4% scenario drops off to $150.6 billion, after adding back net cash as an equivalent for equity. This works out to an equity value of $155.8 billion divided by 2.475 billion shares of diluted shares, giving a share value of $62.94.

Why Palantir Deserves a Premium Multiple Compared to Its Peers

Palantir is priced at much higher forward price/sales 59x than peers such as Salesforce (CRM), Oracle (ORCL), or Snowflake (SNOW) since it's not merely selling software, it's integrating itself as an AI operating system within companies. Whereas most SaaS companies sell tools or dashboards, Palantir drives decision-making itself, automating processes in government, defense, health care, and manufacturing. This equates to every dollar of Palantir revenue having greater strategic value and stickiness, with growing cash flow margins (44% in 2024) and very little customer churn. It's infrastructure, not software.

Conclusion

Palantir's underappreciated ontology framework coupled with monetizing AIP faster leads to its status as the unsung OS of corporate autonomy. With FCF margins in excess of 40%, a balance sheet rich in cash, and government-grade stickiness, it has an asymmetric upside in an AI stack that is likely to commoditize elsewhere. For long-term investors, Palantir's outlook is bullish as a high-conviction stock with a target of ~$175 by 2030.

data.title: Tesla: Upcoming Earnings Are Likely To Disappoint Investors
data.description:
Executive Summary

I think that Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) vehicles look really beautiful and futuristic. However, the picture does not look beautiful if we look at the company from the investor's point of view. TSLA is significantly overvalued, especially considering all the structural and cyclical headwinds it faces. I think there are numerous reasons to expect that the upcoming earnings release will be disappointing, which will likely lead to a sell-off that will narrow the gap between the share price and its fair value. Therefore, TSLA is a strong Sell before the upcoming earnings release.

Earnings Preview Analysis

Tesla's upcoming earnings release is scheduled for April 22, and the company enters the Q1 earnings season facing quite unfavorable structural and cyclical factors. All these factors are not just short-term headwinds, but potentially threaten longer-term competitive positioning as well. Let me walk readers through this bumpy road step-by-step, so my earnings preview is clear and straightforward.

There is a huge red flag named "Tesla's Q1 2025 deliveries". The company's Q1 deliveries dynamic was extremely weak with a 13% year-over-year decline. It is not only very weak by Tesla's own standards, but it also indicates that the company is massively losing its global market share because global EV sales continue demonstrating robust momentum with confident double-digit growth.

A large unfavorable factor for Tesla that significantly undermines its growth potential is relentless competitive pressure from BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY). The comparative line chart under this paragraph speaks volumes, in my opinion. It was not very serious to call BYD a real competitor for Tesla only a few years ago because there was a huge gap in deliveries. BYD was not only able to close the gap but also continues to demonstrate an aggressive upward trend while Tesla's deliveries stagnate.

BYD looks much better prepared for future growth from several perspectives. The company has a much broader product mix, which includes hybrids and really cheap models like Seagull (price below $10,000) and Dolphin (price below $15,000). Thus, even if Tesla successfully starts mass production of its cheaper model with a selling price of $25,000, it will still be substantially more expensive compared to BYD's cheapest models. With that being said, Tesla is unlikely to succeed by trying to compete in pricing (i.e., not a cost leader).

Tesla also experiences problems with differentiating and exercising pricing power. Just a few years ago, there was a perception that BYD lags behind Tesla technologically. However, the early months of 2025 showed us that this gap is also narrowing rapidly. BYD released autonomous driving as its answer to Tesla's FSD in February, which is cheaper. In addition, there is the ultra-fast charging solution from BYD, which also significantly boosts BYD models' attractiveness to consumers. These two releases significantly undermined Tesla's technological moat because its Supercharger and the FSD were two critical differentiation points.

Relentless competitive pressure from BYD not only undermines Tesla's market share (because its deliveries are tanking while the global EV market is thriving) but also puts massive pressure on Tesla's profitability. Tesla's operating margin started tanking in early 2023 when the company started its aggressive price war in China. After more than two years have passed since the inception of the price war against BYD, we can conclude that it was a strategic lapse. The price war did not help to revitalize Tesla's impressive delivery growth and also made the company significantly less profitable.

Apart from struggling to protect its moat against BYD, there is another big problem that is likely to weigh on the management's optimism for the remaining part of 2025. There is an extremely high level of uncertainty around the future of the U.S. economy with new tariffs, but it is highly likely that many manufacturers will pass on higher tariffs' effect to consumers. This will certainly be a robust catalyst to boost inflation, meaning that the Fed is highly unlikely to be aggressive with interest rate cuts. Higher inflation and expensive car loans will highly likely undermine consumers' ability to refresh their cars more frequently. Increased U.S. recession probability is also unlikely to add aggressiveness to consumer spending patterns.

Therefore, it is no surprise that Wall Street analysts keep downgrading their quarterly revenue growth forecasts. This is a notable bearish indicator, and my analysis above suggests that the cautiousness of prominent analysts is 100% justified.

Valuation Is Sky-High

Tesla is well-known for its sky-high multiples, which seemed absolutely okay while the company doubled its deliveries over a few consecutive years while BYD's volumes were not even close to Tesla's. However, my company analysis suggests that there are fundamental reasons why Tesla struggles to drive growth.

Therefore, I absolutely do not share the optimism that is outlined in the above table. It is impossible to agree that the EPS will almost quadruple over the next five years while Tesla's deliveries are tanking (while the entire EV industry is growing) and its operating margin is poised to shrink.

Tesla's FCF in FY2024 was negative. To figure out my FCF assumption, I take the average for the period between 2020 and 2024. It equals $2.974 billion, which I expect to grow approximately in line with current inflation levels. I think it is fair and even somewhat optimistic given Tesla's stagnating deliveries and deteriorating profitability.

The discount rate is calculated above, and 12.54% looks quite fair given TSLA's massive volatility and fundamental risks. I implement an aggressive 4% growth rate into perpetuity because of the thriving EV market, and also to slightly balance out my bearish perspective.

Tesla's fair value (as a company) is around $60 billion. This is less than 10% of the company's current market cap, which many readers might say is unrealistic. However, my fair value estimate looks quite reasonable if we cross-check via valuation ratios.

If we recalculate Tesla's fair value estimate by multiplying its TTM revenue by the sector median P/S ratio, it is around $77 billion (10 times lower than the current market cap). Recalculating Tesla's fair value based on the P/B ratio gives a better outcome with a $136 billion value, but it still indicates overvaluation multiple times. Therefore, Tesla's valuation is absolutely disconnected from its fundamental struggles.

Potential Drawbacks

Some people recommend not to "bet against Elon" because Tesla's CEO undoubtedly has a proven track record of generating excitement with bold product unveilings and aspirational roadmaps. If he presents an eye-catchy prototype of Model Q (or Model 2) and frames any mass production delays as a deliberate step to “achieve perfection”, investors could interpret this as unparalleled visionary talent instead of planning and execution flaws.

There is also Tesla's Energy division, which experiences robust growth momentum. This growth was almost invisible at the company's consolidated level because the scale was still relatively small. However, Musk might emphasize soaring momentum and outline ambitions in expansion plans. This might move investors' focus from struggles experienced by the core automotive business, which will help mitigate potential after-earnings selloff.

Elon Musk potentially announcing that he is stepping down from his controversial government role (DOGE) and returning his full attention to Tesla might also be absorbed with big optimism. This could reassure investors concerned with political risks, potentially catalyzing a relief rally.

The Verdict

While it might be risky to "bet against Elon" prior to the upcoming earnings release, I think that even his unparalleled presentation talent will not be sufficient to outshine the massive fundamental risks the company faces. The earnings release is highly likely to disappoint, which will be a big negative catalyst for a massively overvalued stock like Tesla. Strong Sell looks like the best-fitting rating for TSLA.

data.title: Short Interest Down For U.S. REITs In March, Up For Advertising REITs
data.description:
Average short interest in US equity real estate investment trusts decreased moderately in March, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

US REITs' short interest fell 3 basis points month over month to 3.7% of shares outstanding on average in March. Advertising REITs had the largest increase in average short interest among property types, continuing the trend from February.

Advertising REITs see short surge

Average short interest in the advertising sector increased 66 basis points from the prior month to 7.2% of shares outstanding as of March 31.

The other retail segment — which consists of single-tenant and outlet centers — had the second-highest average short interest among US equity REITs, at 5.4% of shares outstanding.

The hotel sector came in third, with an average short interest of 5.2% of shares outstanding at the end of March, unchanged from February.

The shopping center segment logged the biggest decline in March compared to the previous month, down 1.2 percentage points to 3.3% of shares outstanding on average.

The self-storage sector had the lowest average short interest at 1.8% of shares outstanding, followed by timber sector REITs, which had an average short interest of 1.9% of shares outstanding.

Sweeping changes

As of March 31, hotel REIT Ryman Hospitality Properties Inc. (RHP) posted the largest month-over-month increase in short interest, rising by 4.8 percentage points to 10.0% of shares outstanding.

JBG Smith Properties (JBGS), which registered the largest short interest increase in February, was ranked second in the list of REITs with the largest increase in short interest of shares outstanding in March. The diversified REIT posted a short interest of 14.4% of shares outstanding, up 3.7 percentage points from the previous month.

Single tenant REIT Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) recorded a 6.7% short interest of outstanding shares in March, an increase of 2.6 percentage points compared to February.

Industrial REIT Plymouth Industrial REIT Inc. (PLYM) ended March with short interest at 3.2% of shares outstanding.

In March, the office REITs with the largest increases in short interest of shares outstanding were Kilroy Realty Corp. (KRC), with a 1.8-percentage-point increase to 7.8%; Douglas Emmett Inc. (DEI), with a 1.8-percentage-point increase to 13.0%, making it the third most-shorted REIT overall for the month; Orion Properties Inc. (ONL), with a 1.4-percentage-point increase; and Highwoods Properties Inc. (HIW), with a 1.3-percentage-point increase.

The top three REITs with the steepest percentage declines in short interest of shares outstanding belonged to the shopping center sector.

Acadia Realty Trust (AKR) posted a 7.8-percentage-point decrease in short interest to 4.7% of shares outstanding as of March-end. This was the largest month-over-month drop among REITs.

Wheeler Real Estate Investment Trust Inc. (WHLR) had the second-highest month-over-month decline, with a 4.9-percentage-point fall to 10.7% of shares outstanding in March.

SITE Centers Corp. (SITC) followed with a month-over-month decline of 3.8 percentage points in short interest to 5.4% of shares outstanding in March.

Medical Properties Trust Inc. (MPW) remained the most-shorted US REIT stock in March, but it was also among the REITs with the largest drop in short interest, with a 1.9-percentage-point decline to 28.8% of shares outstanding.

Original Post
