data.title: Weatherford International: Too Cheap (Again) To Ignore
data.description:
Executive Summary

Weatherford International (NASDAQ:WFRD) has improved tremendously since emerging from bankruptcy at the end of 2019 and the company is in a strong position to weather (no pun intended) a potential economic slowdown. The balance sheet is solid, and the company is at the forefront of technological innovations to help reduce customers’ costs by increasing efficiencies. The focus on solutions businesses has improved margins significantly and are now comparable and even higher than peers. The stock has traded down significantly in the last year and the valuation is extremely low. The risk/reward seems to be tilted to the upside despite a tougher oil price environment. I initiate with a Strong Buy and a target of $100/share.

Background

Weatherford International (WFRD) in its current form was formed through bankruptcy in 2019. The company emerged from bankruptcy on December 16, 2019, after shedding most of its debt, $6.2 billion, to be exact, and with a new board. The former bondholders became the owners, and the ownership became more concentrated. The company hired Girish Saligram in September 2020. The management's focus has been on solutions businesses rather than hard equipment assets. They also shed a few businesses before filing for bankruptcy, like its oil-well business in 2017 and the Laboratory Services Business in 2019.

The stock was $1/share in the summer of 2020 and rallied to above $130/share in 2024. The stock is now around $45/share and market capitalization of $3.2 billion.

Ownership and Management

Diluted shares outstanding are 74.5 million. The two largest shareholders are Vanguard and BlackRock with 7.7 million shares and 7.3 million shares respectively, which is 10.3% and 9.8% holdings. The third largest is Capital Research Global Investors at 5.9 million/7.9%, followed by Price T Rowe Associates and T. Rowe Price Investment Management at 5.7 million shares and 4.4 million shares respectively, or 7.6% and 5.9%. Those are the shareholders above 5% holdings. No significant insider ownership or recent activity.

The company's C-suite changed with the balance sheet restructuring in 2019. As mentioned, the CEO, Girish Saligram, was hired in 2020. He came from Exterran Corp. as their COO, which he had been since 2016. Before then, he was in various roles at GE for 20 years. The CFO is Arun Mitra. He joined Weatherford in 2023 with more than 30 years of experience across multiple industries. The board of directors consists of six directors and the CEO is on the board. The board is led by Charles M. (Chuck) Sledge. Among other roles, he serves on the board of Talos Energy, which is another public oil and gas company.

Business Summary

The company’s business has consistently improved since emerging from bankruptcy. Revenue was $3.7 billion in 2020 and $5.5 billion in 2024 and Operating Income improved from negative $44 million to $938 million. EBITDA improved from $459 million to $1281 million, and margins went from 12.5% to 23.2%. Free cash flow for, 2024 was $524mm. The business improvement has led to a better balance sheet as well; debt coming out of bankruptcy was reduced to $2.5 billion from above $8 billion, and is now down to $1.6 billion. The company is now in a good position regarding both its business and its balance sheet.

The company is guiding for $5.1 to $5.35 billion in revenues and $1.2-$1.35 billion in EBITDA for 2025. I forecast ~$5 billion in revenues and $900 million in EBITDA due to lower oil prices and tariff issues since the company put out guidance. The lower oil price will most likely lead to lower activity by oil and gas producers, which are the customers.

Weatherford International is an oil service company that operates through three segments: Drilling and Evaluation, Well Construction and Completions, and Production and Intervention. The company is headquartered in Houston, Texas. The company operates in 75 countries and has 330 operating locations and is one of the largest oil services companies in the world after Schlumberger (SLB), Halliburton (HAL), and Baker Hughes (BKR). The company’s revenues in 2024 from the US were 15%, Mexico 12% (with 10% from one customer, presumably Pemex), Saudi Arabia 10%, and no other country or customer over 10%. Looking at regions, North America is 19%, Latin America 25%, Europe/Sub-Saharan Africa and Russia 17% and Middle-East/North Africa and Asia 38%. The Middle-East segment has grown from 32% in 2022 to now 38% and the US has stagnated from 25% to now 19% of total revenue.

The company has significant exposure to a ramp-up or slowdown in OPEC activity. The company mentioned on its last earnings call that they have seen a slowdown in Russia and in Latin America, which was a big reason to guide for lower revenue in 2025.

The company is investing in R&D, as it is key to have a strong technology offering as oil and gas producers constantly seek to improve and optimize their production. The company’s products and services are designed to enable clients (oil and gas producers/E&P companies) to increase production rates while reducing their costs of drilling and production. All the company’s segments, according to the 10K, “are enabled by a suite of digital monitoring, control, and optimization solutions using advanced analytics to provide safe, reliable and efficient solutions throughout the well life cycle, including responsible abandonment at the end of the well’s productive life.”

The Drilling and Evaluation segment offers various services like managed pressure drilling, drilling services, wireline, and drilling fluids. The company’s services are focused on optimizing reserves and productivity of the oil and gas wells. The segments' revenue was $1682 million in 2024 or 30.5% of total sales and EBITDA was $467 million or 34.6% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 27.8%.

The Well Construction and Completions segment offers products and services throughout the life of the well. The offerings are tubular running services, cementation products, completions, liner hangers, and well services. The company deploys conventional to advanced technologies to keep the process safe and efficient during the well construction phase. The segments' revenue was $1976 million in 2024 or 36% of sales and EBITDA was $564 million or 41.8% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 28.7%.

The Production and Intervention segment offers several reservoir stimulation designs and engineering capabilities to unlock reserves in deep water, old reservoirs, and also in conventional and unconventional wells. The offerings are intervention services and drilling tools, artificial lift, digital solutions, sub-sea intervention, and pressure pumping in select markets. The segments' revenue was $1452 million in 2024 or 26.3% of total sales and EBITDA was $329 million or 24.4% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 22%.

The company also had $403 million in 2024 of Other Sales with EBITDA of $84 million or 6.2% of total EBITDA with an EBITDA margin of 20.8%.

The company invests in R&D over $100 million per year. These are some of the technological achievements the company highlighted in the last earnings press release.

“Technology Highlights

On January 14, 2025, at the annual IKTVA forum held at Dahan Dharan Expo, Weatherford signed an agreement with SPARK, a fully integrated industrial ecosystem aimed at making Saudi Arabia a global energy hub. This strategic partnership, aligned with Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030, enhances Weatherford’s local presence, boosts production capabilities, and supports the region’s energy goals. By advancing local content, fostering talent, and driving innovation, Weatherford demonstrates its commitment to economic growth and to supporting Saudi Arabia’s leadership in energy innovation.

Drilling & Evaluation (DRE)

In the North Sea, Weatherford successfully deployed the world’s first Dual Advanced Kickover Tool for Equinor. The unique solution enables gas lift valve replacements in just a single run, which significantly increases efficiency and reduces the cost of conventional systems.

In Saudi Arabia, Weatherford deployed its compact wireline logging tools with shuttle technology to achieve a record total depth for Aramco. This extended reach well features the longest horizontal section, measuring 23,000 feet.

Well Construction and Completions (WCC)

In deepwater Brazil, Weatherford successfully installed the first OptiRoss® RFID Multi-Cycle Sliding Sleeve Valve for a major operator. The system enhances acid stimulation efficiency, improving production and boosting the reservoir’s oil recovery factor.

In the Middle East, Weatherford successfully deployed its market-leading Optimax Tubing Retrievable Safety Valve for an NOC. This deployment enabled gas lift valve replacements in a single run, significantly increasing efficiency and reducing costs compared to conventional systems.

Production and Intervention (PRI)

In the Middle East, Weatherford's Alpha1Go remote re-entry system was deployed for an NOC, optimizing rig site operations by significantly reducing whipstock preparation time and minimizing red-zone exposure. This deployment improved both efficiency and safety, demonstrating the system's effectiveness in facilitating well re-entry operations and real-time team collaboration in various rig environments.

In US land operations, Weatherford successfully deployed its first Reclaim Dual Barrier Plug and Abandon (P&A) system for a major operator. This innovative dual-barrier P&A system safely and reliably abandons wells without the need to pull tubing. By eliminating the requirement for conventional drilling rigs, it significantly reduces costs and minimizes the carbon footprint.”

The balance sheet underwent a restructuring in 2019 and the company cut $6.2 billion in debt. The company has since reduced it from $2.5 billion in 2020 to $1.6 billion in 2024. The free cash flow for 2024 was over $500 million and over $600 million in 2023. The net debt calculation is only 0.5x and the debt is rated just below investment grade at BB- and a positive outlook by S&P Global.

The company recently launched a buyback program of $500 million over the next three years and initiated a 25c quarterly dividend. In the second half of 2024, the company bought back $100 million of stock and looking to buy back $200 million in 2025. The SBC (share-based compensation) program has around 1.5 million shares per year. Buying back $200 million of shares around $40/share would be 5 million shares for a net of 3.5 million share reduction in 2025.

Value Proposition

Weatherford is clearly a stronger company than 5 years ago, with revenue up close to 60%. The company’s margins have also improved significantly, and the company is in the mid-20% EBITDA margins, which are comparable and even higher vs. public peers. The fact that the company has become a high-margin business seems overlooked by the market. The management even indicated they are still on track for high 20s EBITDA margins in a few years. I believe that it might be harder to achieve in a tougher oil price environment, but longer term it should be doable. The company’s focus on value-added technology has really helped with pricing power. I have EBITDA going lower for 2025 but rebounding in 2026. The company will continue to generate strong free cash flow, and the balance sheet will continue to be solid. The lower valuation vs. peers is not justified in my view, and the overall lower industry valuation could change as soon as later this year as the market looks forward through the tariff debacle.

The growth has been strong but might slow down a bit going forward, but even if 2025 is lower, I have topline rebounding to 5-10% going forward. The company grew 18-19% in 2022 and 2023 and slowed down to 7% in 2024.

The multiple historically for the peers has been above 10x EBITDA vs. 6-7x for (WFRD). (HAL) is currently trading at 5x and the 5-year average is 11x; (SLB) is trading at 7x and the 5-year average is 12x; and (BKR) is trading at 9x and the 5-year average is 10x. The most likely reason for (BKR) trading better is its exposure to LNG terminals.

I believe 8x is fair for a globally diversified oil service company with EBITDA margins in the mid-20% and strong free cash flow. EBITDA for 2024 was $1.3 billion and my estimates for 2025 and 2026 are $900 million and $1050 million, respectively. The stock target is therefore $100/share. See below. The stock is currently trading at 3.1x ltm EBITDA and 4.2x 2025 estimated EBITDA and back down to 3.2x 2026 estimated EBITDA.

Catalysts

Continued improved business performance and stock buyback program. The US business, which has lagged behind the international, might improve under the Trump administration's “drill baby drill” slogan. Also, OPEC is telegraphing more production going forward and as the company has close to 40% of revenue from the region, they might benefit from it. The stock might re-rate IF there is no global meltdown from the US tariff regime.

Risks

The biggest risk is the cyclical nature of oil prices and the industry activity around it. OPEC activity is slowing down. The US onshore shale business continues to experience slow activity. A global recession due to the US tariff regime.

Conclusion

The company has improved its business, reduced its balance sheet risk and the company is much different looking vs. pre-bankruptcy. The stock has recently traded down significantly and the valuation is extremely low. The risk/reward despite a tougher environment seems skewed to the upside.

data.title: SLR Investment: Strong 11% Dividend, Weak Overall Performance
data.description:
Investment Thesis

SLR Investment Corp. (Nasdaq: NASDAQ:SLRC) is a highly defensive investment choice that primarily attracts income- and dividend-oriented investors.

The Company is an externally managed BDC, with investment activities overseen by SLR Capital Partners, and has one of the highest-quality portfolios in the sector. However, despite its solid balance sheet, investors appear indifferent.

Although it offers a strong dividend yield of just under 11% (4 × $0.41 for 2024), which for now appears secure, the stock’s weak performance justifies a “HOLD” recommendation, mainly on the grounds that there are more compelling alternatives with similar characteristics.

In fact, SLRC may not even qualify for inclusion in a portfolio on diversification grounds alone.

Stock Overview

A key factor that prevents me from easily considering the stock a buy is its weak share price performance.

Surprisingly, despite posting solid financial metrics, SLRC is not among the market’s “favorite” BDCs, something clearly reflected in its lukewarm 5-year return of just +6%. Even when including dividends, the total return still fails to surpass +75%.

The underperformance of the stock becomes apparent, especially when compared to the total return of the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), which provides a general benchmark for the broader BDC universe, as well as for certain individual BDCs that I view as top-tier picks, such as Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) and Hercules Capital (HTGC).

The half of the return generated by SLRC compared to BIZD makes investors avoid selecting this stock even for portfolio diversification focused on income generation. If the goal was exposure, without emphasizing maximum total return, an investor would prefer BIZD.

To be clear, I am not suggesting that SLRC’s performance is negative, when evaluating its high-quality portfolio and skilled management team (which we’ll examine below), but the stock fails to differentiate itself from peer companies in the sector. I suspect that the company's NAV dynamics might explain this unusual market behavior.

The recent Trump tariff announcements, together with market pressure, caused SLRC stock to decline by 19% from its 2025 peak. The stock reached its three-year peak at a level that stood 32% lower than its previous decade-high point. The current stock price of $15 represents a 100% increase from pandemic lows.

The stock shows a prolonged declining pattern until a strong upward breakout above the three-year peak will start to change investor perceptions about this stock.

A Look at the NAV

As mentioned earlier, SLRC’s NAV picture is a little bizarre. Despite net assets increasing to $992.9 million or $18.20 per share in 2024, this represents only a 0.6% annual growth rate, and the more concerning is that expectations for a meaningful improvement from this stagnant trend remain quite low.

To be more precise, SLRC’s NAV was unchanging in the past decade, with a total growth of just 7% over the last 10 years. While this might suggest a very stable and resilient structure, it also leaves little room for upside potential.

To put this into perspective: ARCC has achieved a NAV increase of +153%, MAIN +156%, and HTGC +168% over the same period.

Therefore, no matter how high the quality of SLRC’s portfolio may be, its weak track record in terms of growth may prevent long-term investors, while combined with the stock’s gradual downward drift, the generous almost 11% dividend ($0.41 for Q4) simply isn’t enough to compensate.

At current levels, SLRC is trading at a 17% discount to NAV, almost 0.83x, placing it close to the sector-wide average for BDCs.

On the other hand, the “high-quality” names remain at a premium despite ongoing selling pressure: MAIN trades at a 70% premium, HTGC at 50%, and TRIN at 8%.

Incidentally, ARCC is trading at par (1:1), which could present a compelling entry point—but that’s a separate discussion beyond the scope of this article.

One could argue that discounted names like SLRC represent attractive opportunities. My view, however, is that such stocks are priced low or lower for a reason, just as the better-positioned names naturally deserve the premiums they trade at.

Portfolio and Financial Profile

SLR’s portfolio is extremely defensive, with more than 96% of total investments allocated to first-lien loans.

A more detailed analysis shows that about 70% of the portfolio is backed by assets and equipment finance (any tangible collateral other than real estate) which provides additional value and security to the underlying loans because of the quality and recoverability of the pledged assets.

The financial metrics are solid. Net investment income-NII remained stable at $0.44 for Q4 (and $1.77 for the full year 2024), ensuring the $0.41 quarterly dividend.

Even more interesting is SLRC’s relatively low leverage, with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.03x (well below the BDC sector average of 1.18x).

For context, several peers have even lower leverage levels:

Gladstone Capital Corporation (GLAD) at 0.66x, Fidus Investment Corp. (FDUS) at 0.73x, Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) at 0.76x, and Hercules Capital (HTGC) at 0.89x. Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) is almost identical at 1.03x, and Trinity Capital (TRIN) stands slightly higher at 1.06x.

In general, the closer a BDC gets to the statutory limit of 2.0x leverage, the less attractive it becomes. This is because higher leverage reduces flexibility for further borrowing and increases the company’s sensitivity to adverse conditions, whether from portfolio-specific stress events or broader market disruptions.

Conclusion

From all the above, it becomes clear that SLRC’s biggest weakness lies in its low growth expectations.

In broad terms, there’s not much, at least theoretically, that it lacks compared to the "best-in-class" BDCs, and frankly, it’s hard to imagine an investor actually losing money on this type of position.

In a less competitive field, SLRC would likely stand out as an excellent option in its own right. That’s precisely why I maintain a hold recommendation.

The dividend is secure and quite generous (just under 11%) and the quality of the portfolio helps shield the company from unfortunate events that could otherwise cause harm.

Unfortunately, it seems investors are not as drawn to the management’s conservative approach, as it reduces the likelihood of outsized gains through capital appreciation.

data.title: Stock Market Swoon Could Hit Some P&C Insurers Harder Than Others
data.description:
The investment portfolios of US property and casualty insurers currently stand to take a hit from the recent global sell-off in equities, but the magnitude of the impact on the vast majority of the companies in the sector is significantly inflated by one group's uniquely large allocation to common stocks.

Holdings in unaffiliated publicly traded common stocks among US P&C insurers hit $440.35 billion as of year-end 2024, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of disclosures on Schedule D, Part 2, Section 2 of annual statutory filings, an amount that accounted for 17.7% of net admitted cash and invested assets.

The majority of the industry's exposure to publicly traded stocks sits within the subsidiaries of a single group, however, as the P&C subsidiaries of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) reported holding $249.61 billion. While that value declined substantially from $323.13 billion a year earlier, reflecting the company's much-discussed shift to cash and short-term investments, it still was more than twice as large as any other US P&C group's position. Berkshire and the group led by State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Co. had 47.6% and 39.6% of their net admitted cash and invested assets allocated to unaffiliated publicly traded common equities. The rest of the industry's combined position amounted to just 5.1% of cash and invested assets, though there were several outliers with outsized concentrations in common equities.

If we were to hypothetically assume a drop in valuations in the holdings of P&C insurers excluding Berkshire equivalent to the S&P 500 Index's year-to-date decline of 13.2% through the April 7 market close, this would suggest a reduction in aggregate policyholders' surplus through a corresponding negative change in unrealized capital gains and losses of approximately $26.61 billion, or about 2.4% of the industry's Dec. 31, 2024, total.

With much of the market sell-off transpiring after the start of the second quarter, it will be months before any effects would manifest themselves in the form of hits to insurer balance sheets and capital-and-surplus accounts. But the underlying factors behind the market woes, including an elevated potential for an economic downturn, fears of significant inflation in consumer and producer prices, and diminished prospects for earnings-per-share growth, may present more of a clear and present threat to the industry.

Most P&C insurers traditionally concentrate a majority of their investments in investment-grade corporate bonds with significant allocations to securities issued by government agencies and the US federal government. Combined, those three asset classes accounted for nearly 52.9% of the industry's net admitted cash and invested assets at year-end 2024 when excluding Berkshire. Of the 1,132 US P&C groups and stand-alone P&C entities with data available as of April 7, there were 616 that showed no investment in unaffiliated, publicly traded common stocks whatsoever as of Dec. 31, 2024. Another 301 held some investments in publicly traded equities but at an amount that constituted less than 10% of their net admitted cash and invested assets.

We examined the top 25 unaffiliated, publicly traded common stocks held by P&C insurers other than Berkshire as of year-end 2024 by both the aggregate carrying value of the industry's positions and the number of P&C groups and stand-alone entities that held individual stocks. In both lists, the number of stocks that declined on a year-to-date basis, not surprisingly, far exceeded the count of those that appreciated in value though performances varied widely.

Shares of Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), the industry's largest investment in an unaffiliated, publicly traded common stock by carrying value, fell by nearly 6.3% through the April 7 close, outperforming the S&P 500. But shares of Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), the industry's most widely owned name by number of holders, tumbled by 15.1% over the same timeframe. Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) shares, which would have easily topped the list of industry holdings by carrying value had Berkshire's mammoth position been incorporated in our analysis, plunged by 27.5% on a year-to-date basis through April 7.

The top-performing stock on each of the top lists was Abbott Laboratories, though its year-to-date gain of 10.4% through the April 7 close had marked a significant retreat from an early March peak. The industry's largest Abbott Labs (ABT) investors as of Dec. 31, 2024, were State Farm and Cincinnati Financial Corp.

At the other end of the spectrum, Broadcom Inc.'s (AVGO) 33.5% year-to-date decline was the largest among stocks with the broadest and deepest US P&C industry investment. State Farm along with the group led by Factory Mutual Insurance Co. and The Cincinnati held the largest positions by carrying value in the semiconductor developer's stock. Common stocks accounted for 46.7% and30.5%, respectively, of the year-end 2024 unaffiliated investments held by FM Global, the name by which Factory Mutual conducts business, and The Cincinnati. Other notable P&C insurers overweight equities relative to the industry as a whole include Auto Club Exchange Group, Markel Group Inc. (MKL) and the respective groups led by Palisades Safety & Insurance Association and Merrimack Mutual Fire Insurance Co.

The latter of those entities, which operate in combination with an affiliate and subsidiary as the Andover Companies, said in its 2024 annual statement that it has focused its portfolio on dividend-paying common stocks due to their track record of providing "significant cash flow over the last 10 years in a historically low interest rate environment." The company added that it maintains a "high quality" bond portfolio and conservative cash balances to avoid being put in a position where it might have to unload common stock during "inopportune periods" when equity prices are under pressure.

P&C insurers held relatively low levels of exposure to the S&P 500 constituents with the largest month-to-date share price declines through April 7. Among the 17 issues with drops of 20% or more in the first seven days of April, the combined Dec. 31, 2024, carrying value of the industry's holdings was less than $71.4 million. Positions in Zebra Technologies Corp. (ZBRA), which has plunged by 42.1% for the year and 20.9% for the month to date, accounted for $18.9 million of that tally.

These prospectively temporary declines in valuations are unlikely to have a significant impact on insurance company holders with large investment portfolios that are diversified across asset classes and strategies. But there are several smaller P&C insurers with outsized exposure to common equities.

Merchants Property Insurance Co. of Indiana, whose investments in unaffiliated, publicly traded common equities amounted to 102.3% of its year-end 2023 policyholders' surplus, might have the most interesting perspective on the situation.

A former homeowners and commercial package policy insurer that has been in run-off for more than 20 years, Indianapolis-based Merchants Property Insurance held 94 publicly traded common equities, including four positions that date back to 1937 and nine others that it originally acquired between 1949 and 1953. The latter investments, the company said in its 2024 annual statement, had been purchased as a hedge against post-World War II inflation. The strategy, it added, "has proven rewarding in the ensuing decades."

While Merchants Property Insurance indicated that it had taken some capital gains on its common stock positions in 2024 to "take advantage of a low tax climate," if we were to assume that its share counts in all 94 equities held steady on a year-to-date basis through April 7, the corresponding fair value decline would amount to an estimated $10.9 million, or 8.7% from the year-end total.

For most P&C insurers, the recent market declines could make a small dent in relatively large unrealized gain positions they had amassed. The aggregate carrying value of unaffiliated publicly traded common shares held by P&C insurers excluding Berkshire exceeded actual cost by $125.57 billion as of Dec. 31, 2024, up from $114.09 billion a year earlier. Of the groups and stand-alone entities that held unaffiliated, publicly traded common stocks as of year-end 2024, 85.6% showed carrying values that were higher than the actual cost of those investments.

The P&C industry, with and without Berkshire, produced a new high in realized capital gains on investments in unaffiliated common stock of $106.05 billion and $11.82 billion, respectively. In theory, this could provide dry powder for insurers to take advantage of buying opportunities left in the wake of the recent sell-off, though in some cases, more than a quarter of the way into 2025, the beneficiaries have already reinvested those proceeds elsewhere.
